1769	__global__ void layernorm_shift_partition_COL32_warpReduce(int8_t*       out_ptr,
1770	                                                           const __half* input_ptr,
1771	                                                           const __half* gamma_ptr,
1772	                                                           const __half* beta_ptr,
1773	                                                           int           batch,
1774	                                                           int           H,
1775	                                                           int           W,
1776	                                                           int           n,
1777	                                                           const float   qScale,
1778	                                                           int           shift_size,
1779	                                                           int           window_size)
1780	{
1781	    const int lane_id   = threadIdx.x % 32;
1782	    const int warp_id   = threadIdx.x / 32;
1783	    const int col_start = lane_id * VPT;
1784	    const int row_id    = blockIdx.x * WPB + warp_id;
1785	    if (row_id >= batch * H * W || col_start >= n)
1786	        return;
1787	
1788	    const int blockIdx_z = row_id / (H * W);
1789	    const int blockIdx_y = (row_id / W) % H;
1790	    const int blockIdx_x = row_id % W;
1791	
1792	    const int batch_offset = blockIdx_z * H * W;
1793	    const int m            = batch * H * W;
1794	
1795	    const int shifted_H_idx = (shift_size != 0) ? ((blockIdx_y - shift_size + H) % H) : blockIdx_y;
1796	    const int shifted_W_idx = (shift_size != 0) ? ((blockIdx_x - shift_size + W) % W) : blockIdx_x;
1797	    const int window_H_idx  = shifted_H_idx / window_size;
1798	    const int window_W_idx  = shifted_W_idx / window_size;
1799	    const int window_idx    = window_H_idx * (W / window_size) + window_W_idx;
1800	    const int idx_in_window = (shifted_H_idx % window_size) * window_size + (shifted_W_idx % window_size);
1801	    const int output_bid    = batch_offset + window_idx * window_size * window_size + idx_in_window;
1802	
1803	    const int idx    = (col_start & 0xffffffe0) * m + (row_id << 5) + (col_start & 31);
1804	    const int outIdx = (col_start & 0xffffffe0) * m + (output_bid << 5) + (col_start & 31);
1805	
1806	    float  in_local_dq[VPT];
1807	    __half in_local[VPT];
1808	    __half beta_local[VPT];
1809	    __half gamma_local[VPT];
1810	    copy<sizeof(__half) * VPT>(&input_ptr[idx], in_local);
1811	    copy<sizeof(__half) * VPT>(&gamma_ptr[col_start], gamma_local);
1812	    copy<sizeof(__half) * VPT>(&beta_ptr[col_start], beta_local);
1813	
1814	    float local_sum = 0.0f;
1815	#pragma unroll
1816	    for (int it = 0; it < VPT; it++) {
1817	        in_local_dq[it] = in_local[it];
1818	        local_sum += in_local_dq[it];
1819	    }
1820	
1821	    __shared__ float mu[WPB];
1822	    __shared__ float rsigma[WPB];
1823	
1824	    local_sum = warpReduceSum<float>(local_sum);
1825	
1826	    if (lane_id == 0) {
1827	        mu[warp_id] = local_sum * __fdividef(1.0f, n);
1828	    }
1829	    __syncthreads();
1830	
1831	    local_sum = 0.0f;
1832	#pragma unroll
1833	    for (int it = 0; it < VPT; it++) {
1834	        local_sum += (in_local_dq[it] - mu[warp_id]) * (in_local_dq[it] - mu[warp_id]);
1835	    }
1836	    local_sum = warpReduceSum<float>(local_sum);
1837	    if (lane_id == 0) {
1838	        rsigma[warp_id] = rsqrtf(local_sum * __fdividef(1.0f, n) + 1e-5);
1839	    }
1840	    __syncthreads();
1841	
1842	    static_assert(VPT % 4 == 0, "");
1843	    uint32_t out_local[VPT / 4];
1844	#pragma unroll
1845	    for (int it = 0; it < VPT; it++) {
1846	        const float tmp0 =
1847	            (in_local_dq[it * 4 + 0] - mu[warp_id]) * rsigma[warp_id] * static_cast<float>(gamma_local[it * 4 + 0])
1848	            + static_cast<float>(beta_local[it * 4 + 0]);
1849	        const float tmp1 =
1850	            (in_local_dq[it * 4 + 1] - mu[warp_id]) * rsigma[warp_id] * static_cast<float>(gamma_local[it * 4 + 1])
1851	            + static_cast<float>(beta_local[it * 4 + 1]);
1852	        const float tmp2 =
1853	            (in_local_dq[it * 4 + 2] - mu[warp_id]) * rsigma[warp_id] * static_cast<float>(gamma_local[it * 4 + 2])
1854	            + static_cast<float>(beta_local[it * 4 + 2]);
1855	        const float tmp3 =
1856	            (in_local_dq[it * 4 + 3] - mu[warp_id]) * rsigma[warp_id] * static_cast<float>(gamma_local[it * 4 + 3])
1857	            + static_cast<float>(beta_local[it * 4 + 3]);
1858	        out_local[it] = float4_to_char4(tmp0 * qScale, tmp1 * qScale, tmp2 * qScale, tmp3 * qScale);
1859	    }
1860	
1861	    copy<sizeof(int8_t) * VPT>(out_local, &out_ptr[outIdx]);
1862	}
1863	
1864	template<int VPT>
1865	__global__ void layernorm_shift_partition_COL32(int8_t*       out_ptr,
1866	                                                const __half* input_ptr,
1867	                                                const __half* gamma_ptr,
1868	                                                const __half* beta_ptr,
1869	                                                int           batch,
1870	                                                int           H,
1871	                                                int           W,
1872	                                                int           n,
1873	                                                const float   qScale,
1874	                                                int           shift_size,
1875	                                                int           window_size)
1876	{
1877	    const int batch_offset = blockIdx.z * gridDim.y * gridDim.x;
1878	    const int m            = gridDim.z * gridDim.y * gridDim.x;
1879	    const int bid          = batch_offset + blockIdx.y * gridDim.x + blockIdx.x;
1880	
1881	    const int shifted_H_idx      = (shift_size != 0) ? ((blockIdx.y - shift_size + gridDim.y) % gridDim.y) : blockIdx.y;
1882	    const int shifted_W_idx      = (shift_size != 0) ? ((blockIdx.x - shift_size + gridDim.x) % gridDim.x) : blockIdx.x;
1883	    const int window_H_idx       = shifted_H_idx / window_size;
1884	    const int window_W_idx       = shifted_W_idx / window_size;
1885	    const int stride_of_window_H = W / window_size;
1886	    const int window_idx         = window_H_idx * stride_of_window_H + window_W_idx;
1887	    const int idx_in_window      = (shifted_H_idx % window_size) * window_size + (shifted_W_idx % window_size);
1888	    const int output_bid         = batch_offset + window_idx * window_size * window_size + idx_in_window;
1889	
1890	    int tid = threadIdx.x * VPT;
1891	    if (tid >= n)
1892	        return;
1893	    __shared__ float mu;
1894	    __shared__ float rsigma;
1895	    __half           inputTmp[VPT];
1896	    __half           beta_local[VPT];
1897	    __half           gamma_local[VPT];
1898	    float            in_local_dq[VPT];
1899	
1900	    const int offset_col32 = (tid & 0xffffffe0) * m + (bid << 5) + (tid & 31);
1901	    copy<sizeof(__half) * VPT>(&input_ptr[offset_col32], inputTmp);
1902	    copy<sizeof(__half) * VPT>(&gamma_ptr[tid], gamma_local);
1903	    copy<sizeof(__half) * VPT>(&beta_ptr[tid], beta_local);
1904	
1905	    float local_sum = 0.0f;
1906	#pragma unroll
1907	    for (int it = 0; it < VPT; it++) {
1908	        in_local_dq[it] = inputTmp[it];
1909	        local_sum += in_local_dq[it];
1910	    }
1911	
1912	    local_sum = blockReduceSum<float>(local_sum);
1913	
1914	    if (threadIdx.x == 0) {
1915	        mu = local_sum * __fdividef(1.0f, n);
1916	    }
1917	    __syncthreads();
1918	
1919	    local_sum = 0.0f;
1920	#pragma unroll
1921	    for (int it = 0; it < VPT; it++) {
1922	        local_sum += (in_local_dq[it] - mu) * (in_local_dq[it] - mu);
1923	    }
1924	    local_sum = blockReduceSum<float>(local_sum);
1925	    if (threadIdx.x == 0) {
1926	        rsigma = rsqrtf(local_sum * __fdividef(1.0f, n) + 1e-5);
1927	    }
1928	    __syncthreads();
1929	
1930	    static_assert(VPT % 4 == 0, "");
1931	    uint32_t out_local[VPT / 4];
1932	    if (tid < n) {
1933	#pragma unroll
1934	        for (int it = 0; it < VPT / 4; it++) {
1935	            const float tmp0 = (in_local_dq[it * 4 + 0] - mu) * rsigma * static_cast<float>(gamma_local[it * 4 + 0])
1936	                               + static_cast<float>(beta_local[it * 4 + 0]);
1937	            const float tmp1 = (in_local_dq[it * 4 + 1] - mu) * rsigma * static_cast<float>(gamma_local[it * 4 + 1])
1938	                               + static_cast<float>(beta_local[it * 4 + 1]);
1939	            const float tmp2 = (in_local_dq[it * 4 + 2] - mu) * rsigma * static_cast<float>(gamma_local[it * 4 + 2])
1940	                               + static_cast<float>(beta_local[it * 4 + 2]);
1941	            const float tmp3 = (in_local_dq[it * 4 + 3] - mu) * rsigma * static_cast<float>(gamma_local[it * 4 + 3])
1942	                               + static_cast<float>(beta_local[it * 4 + 3]);
1943	            out_local[it] = float4_to_char4(tmp0 * qScale, tmp1 * qScale, tmp2 * qScale, tmp3 * qScale);
1944	        }
1945	        const int outIdx = (tid & 0xffffffe0) * m + (output_bid << 5) + (tid & 31);
1946	        copy<sizeof(int8_t) * VPT>(out_local, &out_ptr[outIdx]);
1947	    }
1948	}

[Single GPU BERT performance](#single-gpu-bert-performance) 24 - [BERT performance on A100 and TensorFlow](#bert-performance-on-a100-and-tensorflow) 25 - [BERT performance on T4 and TensorFlow](#bert-performance-on-t4-and-tensorflow) 26 - [BERT performance on V100 and TensorFlow](#bert-performance-on-v100-and-tensorflow) 27 - [BERT performance comparison between T4, V100, A100 and A100 with MIG mode on TensorFlow](#bert-performance-comparison-between-t4-v100-a100-and-a100-with-mig-mode-on-tensorflow) 28 - [BERT performance comparison between different features on T4 and TensorFlow](#bert-performance-comparison-between-different-features-on-t4-and-tensorflow) 29 - [BERT performance on A100 and PyTorch](#bert-performance-on-a100-and-pytorch) 30 - [BERT performance on A10 and PyTorch](#bert-performance-on-a10-and-pytorch) 31 - [BERT performance on T4 and PyTorch](#bert-performance-on-t4-and-pytorch) 32 - [BERT performance on V100 and PyTorch](#bert-performance-on-v100-and-pytorch) 33 - [Performance on BERT Applications: SQuAD MRPC](#performance-on-bert-applications-squad-mrpc) 34 - [Performance of TensorFlow](#performance-of-tensorflow) 35 - [Performance of PyTorch](#performance-of-pytorch) 36 37 ## Model architecture 38 39 ### Standard BERT and Effective FasterTransformer 40 41 The following configurations are supported in the FasterTransformer encoder. 42 - Batch size (B1): smaller or equal to 4096 43 - Sequence length (S): smaller or equal to 4096. For INT8 mode=1, S should be a multiple of 32 when S > 384. 44 - Size per head (N): Even number and smaller than 128. 45 - Head number (H): Any number satisfying that H * N <= 1024 under FP32, or H * N <= 2048 under FP16. 46 - Data type: FP32, FP16, BF16, INT8 and FP8 (**Experimental**). 47 - Any number layer (N1) if the memory is enough 48 49 In the FasterTransformer v1.0, we provide a highly optimized BERT-equivalent encoder model. Next, based on the idea of [Effective Transformer](https://github.com/bytedance/effective_transformer), we further optimize BERT inference by removing the useless padding in FasterTransformer v2.1 and provide the Effective FasterTransformer. In FasterTransformer v3.0, we provide INT8 quantization inference to get better performance. In FasterTransformer v3.1, we optimize the INT8 kernels to improve the performance of INT8 inference and integrate the multi-head attention of TensorRT plugin into FasterTransformer. In FasterTransformer v4.0, we add the multi-head attention kernel to support FP16 on V100 and INT8 on T4, A100. The following graph demonstrates the flow chart of these optimization, except INT8. In FasterTransformer v5.0, we refactor the codes, encapsulating the mask building and padding removing into the Bert forward function, and add the sparsity feature of Ampere GPU to accelerate the GEMM. In FasterTransformer v5.1, we support multi-node multi-GPU inference on Bert FP16. 50 51
52
Fig. 1 Flowchart of encoder.
53

54 55 The BERT model is proposed by google in 2018. The encoder of FasterTransformer is equivalent to BERT model, but do lots of optimization. The leftmost flow of Fig. 1 shows the optimization in FasterTransformer. After optimization, FasterTransformer only uses 8 or 6 gemms (blue blocks) and 6 custom CUDA kernels (green blocks) to implement one transformer block. 56 57 For Effective FasterTransformer, the main idea is removing the padding of sentence to prevent computing the useless tokens. This method can save lots of time when the average sequence length of one batch is much smaller than the maximum sequence length. Fig. 2 shows the idea and the offset (in orange) we use. To implement the Effective FasterTransformer, we need to consider two problems. First, we need to remove the padding before BERT, and rebuild padding after leaving BERT to keep the shape of result. This is simple and only bring ignorable overhead. The second problem is the computing of multi-head attention. A naïve solution is rebuilding the padding before the multi-head attention and removing the padding after the multi-head attention, which demonstrate in the second flowchart of Fig. 1. Because we can fuse these rebuilding/removing into other kernels, the additional overheads are also ignorable. 58 59 To further improve the performance of multi head attention, we integrate the multi-head attention of TensorRT, which fuses the whole attention computing into one kernel. The source codes are [here]( https://github.com/NVIDIA/TensorRT/tree/master/plugin/bertQKVToContextPlugin). This kernel supports Effective FasterTransformer and standard BERT model at the same time. The third and forth flowcharts in Fig. 1 shows the workflow. With such kernel, we do not worry the padding issue multi-head attention. This kernel requires another offset, which is also show in Fig. 2. 60 61
62
Fig. 2 Effective Transformer.
63

64 65 Besides, we find that the padding would affect the accuracy for some tasks although they should be useless. So, we recommend removing the padding in the final outputs of downstream tasks. 66 67 The arguments, inputs, and outputs of encoder: 68 69 * Constructor of BERT 70 71 | Classification | Name | Data Type | Description | 72 | :------------: | :--------------------------: | :----------------: | :-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------: | 73 | [0] | max_batch_size | int | **Deprecated, move to input** | 74 | [1] | max_seq_len | int | **Deprecated, move to input** | 75 | [2] | head_num | int | Head number for model configuration | 76 | [3] | size_per_head | int | Size per head for model configuration | 77 | [4] | inter_size | int | The inter size of feed forward network. It is often set to 4 * head_num * size_per_head. | 78 | [5] | num_layer | int | Number of transformer layers for model configuration | 79 | [6] | sm | int | The compute capacity of GPU | 80 | [7] | q_scaling | float | It is used to scale the query before the batch multiplication of query and key | 81 | [8] | stream | cudaStream_t | CUDA stream | 82 | [9] | cublas_wrapper | cublasMMWrapper* | Pointer of cuBLAS wrapper, which is declared in `src/fastertransformer/utils/cublasMMWrapper.h` | 83 | [10] | allocator | IAllocator* | Pointer of memory allocator, which is declared in `src/fastertransformer/utils/allocator.h` | 84 | [11] | is_free_buffer_after_forward | bool | If setting to be `true`, FasterTransformer will allocate buffer before forward, and free buffer after forward. When the allocator is based on memory pool, setting to `true` may help reducing the memory usage during inference. | 85 | [12] | attention_type | AttentionType | Determine fusing the attention or not, remove padding or not, which is declared in `src/fastertransformer/layers/attention_layers/BaseAttentionLayer.h` | 86 | [13] | sparse | bool | Is using sparsity. **Experimental feature** | 87 | [14] | activation_type | ActivationType | Determine the activation in FFN, which is declared in `src/fastertransformer/layers/attention_layers/FfnLayer.h` | 88 | [15] | layernorm_type | LayerNormType | Determine using pre-layernorm or post-layernorm, which is declared in `src/fastertransformer/kernels/layernorm_kernels.h` | 89 | [16] | tensor_para | NcclParam | Tensor Parallel information, which is declared in `src/fastertransformer/utils/nccl_utils.h` | 90 | [17] | pipeline_para | NcclParam | Pipeline Parallel information, which is declared in `src/fastertransformer/utils/nccl_utils.h` | 91 | [18] | custom_all_reduce_comm | AbstractCustomComm | Custom all reduction communication for custom all reduction in model parallelism. It is only supported in 8-way tensor parallelism | 92 | [19] | enable_custom_all_reduce | int | Flag of enabling custom all reduction or not | 93 94 * Input of BERT 95 96 | Name | Tensor/Parameter Shape | Location | Data Type | Description | 97 | :----------------: | :-----------------------------------------------------: | :------: | :------------: | :-------------------------------: | 98 | input_hidden_state | [batch_size, sequence_length, head_num * size_per_head] | GPU | fp32/fp16/bf16 | The input of transformer layer | 99 | input_lengths | [batch_size] | GPU | int | The lengths of input_hidden_state | 100 101 * Output of BERT 102 103 | Name | Tensor/Parameter Shape | Location | Data Type | Description | 104 | :-----------------: | :-----------------------------------------------------: | :------: | :------------: | :-----------------------------: | 105 | output_hidden_state | [batch_size, sequence_length, head_num * size_per_head] | GPU | fp32/fp16/bf16 | The output of transformer layer | 106 107 Besides, notice that the multi-head attention kernel from TensorRT is powerful but have some limitation. First, this kernel requires Turing or new GPU and the size per head must be 64. When the conditions are not satisfied, we use original multi-head attention implementation of FasterTransformer. Second, it requires an additional sequence length offset like fig 2 shows. More details are in [link](https://github.com/NVIDIA/TensorRT/tree/release/7.2/plugin/embLayerNormPlugin). When the input has padding, the shape of the sequence length offset is \[2 x B1 + 1 \]. Assume there are three sentences with sequence length s1, s2 and s3, and the sequence length after padding is S. Then the sequence length offset is \[0, s1, S, s2 + S, 2 x S, 2 x S + s3, 3 x S\]. On the other hand, when we remove the padding, the shape of the sequence length offset is \[B1 + 1\], and the sequence length offset is \[0, s1, s1 + s2, s1 + s2 + s3 \]. Namely, the sequence length offset records the sequence length for each sentence. When we have padding, we view the padding as some independent sentences. 108 109 In FasterTransformer v4.0, we implement two pipelines of INT8 inference, as shown in Fig. 3. For int8_mode == 1 (int8v1), we don't quantize residual connection, use int32 as the output of int8 gemms and use per-channel quantization for weights. For int8_mode == 2 (int8v2), we quantize residual connection, use int8 as the output of int8 gemms and use per-tensor quantization for weights. Generally speaking, int8_mode == 1 will have higher accuracy while int8_mode == 2 will have better performance. 110 111
112
Fig. 3 Workflow of int8 inference.
113

114 115 | feature | int8_mode == 1 | int8_mode == 2 | 116 | :---------------------------------: | :------------: | :------------: | 117 | quantize residual | No | Yes | 118 | int8 output gemm | No | Yes | 119 | per-channel quantiztion for weights | Yes | No | 120 121 For INT8 inference, quantized model is needed. We provide TensorFlow quantization tool and sample codes, and PyTorch sample codes with TensorRT's quantization tool is also provided. Please refer to the `README` in `bert-quantization/bert-tf-quantization` and `examples/pytorch/bert/bert-quantization-sparsity` first. 122 123 In FasterTransformer v5.0, we support the sparsity gemm to leverage the sparsity feature of Ampere GPU. We also provide an example on PyTorch. 124 125 In FasterTransformer v5.1, we support the multi-GPU multi-node inference for BERT model. 126 127 ## Setup 128 129 The following section lists the requirements to use FasterTransformer BERT. 130 131 ### Requirements 132 133 - CMake >= 3.8 for Tensorflow, CMake >= 3.13 for PyTorch 134 - CUDA 11.0 or newer version 135 - Python: Only verify on python 3 136 - Tensorflow: Verify on 1.15, 1.13 and 1.14 should work. 137 - PyTorch: Verify on 1.8.0, >= 1.5.0 should work. 138 139 These components are readily available within the NGC TensorFlow/PyTorch Docker image below. 140 141 Ensure you have the following components: 142 - [NVIDIA Docker](https://github.com/NVIDIA/nvidia-docker) and NGC container are recommended 143 - [NVIDIA Pascal](https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/data-center/pascal-gpu-architecture/) or [Volta](https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/data-center/volta-gpu-architecture/) or [Turing](https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/geforce/turing/) or [Ampere](https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/data-center/nvidia-ampere-gpu-architecture/) based GPU 144 145 For more information about how to get started with NGC containers, see the following sections from the NVIDIA GPU Cloud Documentation and the Deep Learning Documentation: 146 147 - [Getting Started Using NVIDIA GPU Cloud](https://docs.nvidia.com/ngc/ngc-getting-started-guide/index.html) 148 - [Accessing And Pulling From The NGC Container Registry](https://docs.nvidia.com/deeplearning/frameworks/user-guide/index.html#accessing_registry) 149 - [Running TensorFlow](https://docs.nvidia.com/deeplearning/frameworks/tensorflow-release-notes/running.html#running) 150 - [Running PyTorch](https://docs.nvidia.com/deeplearning/frameworks/pytorch-release-notes/index.html) 151 152 For those unable to use the NGC container, to set up the required environment or create your own container, see the versioned [NVIDIA Container Support Matrix](https://docs.nvidia.com/deeplearning/frameworks/support-matrix/index.html). 153 154 ### Build the FasterTransformer 155 156 #### Prepare 157 158 You can choose the tensorflow version and python version you want. Here, we list some possible images: 159 160 To achieve best performance, we recommend to use the latest image. For example, running image `nvcr.io/nvidia/tensorflow:22.09-tf1-py3` by 161 162 ```bash 163 nvidia-docker run -ti --shm-size 5g --rm nvcr.io/nvidia/tensorflow:22.09-tf1-py3 bash 164 git clone https://github.com/NVIDIA/FasterTransformer.git 165 mkdir -p FasterTransformer/build 166 cd FasterTransformer/build 167 git submodule init && git submodule update 168 ``` 169 170 For pytorch, it is similar 171 172 ```bash 173 nvidia-docker run -ti --shm-size 5g --rm nvcr.io/nvidia/pytorch:22.09-py3 bash 174 git clone https://github.com/NVIDIA/FasterTransformer.git 175 mkdir -p FasterTransformer/build 176 cd FasterTransformer/build 177 git submodule init && git submodule update 178 ``` 179 180 #### Build the project 181 182 * Note: the `xx` of `-DSM=xx` in following scripts means the compute capability of your GPU. The following table shows the compute capability of common GPUs. 183 184 | GPU | compute capacity | 185 | :---: | :--------------: | 186 | P40 | 60 | 187 | P4 | 61 | 188 | V100 | 70 | 189 | T4 | 75 | 190 | A100 | 80 | 191 | A30 | 80 | 192 | A10 | 86 | 193 194 By default, `-DSM` is set by 70, 75, 80 and 86. When users set more kinds of `-DSM`, it requires longer time to compile. So, we suggest setting the `-DSM` for the device you use only. Here, we use `xx` as an example due to convenience. 195 196 1. build with C++ 197 198 ```bash 199 cmake -DSM=xx -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release .. 200 make -j12 201 ``` 202 203 2. build with TensorFlow 204 205 Uses need to set the path of TensorFlow. For example, if we use `nvcr.io/nvidia/tensorflow:22.09-tf1-py3`, then 206 207 ```bash 208 cmake -DSM=xx -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release -DBUILD_TF=ON -DTF_PATH=/usr/local/lib/python3.8/dist-packages/tensorflow_core/ .. 209 make -j12 210 ``` 211 212 3. build with PyTorch 213 214 ```bash 215 cmake -DSM=xx -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release -DBUILD_PYT=ON -DBUILD_MULTI_GPU=ON .. 216 make -j12 217 ``` 218 219 This will build the TorchScript custom class. Please make sure that the `PyTorch >= 1.5.0`. 220 221 If user want to use sparsity gemm on BERT, then you need to download and extract the cusparseLt library and build with `-DSPARSITY_SUPPORT=ON -DCUSPARSELT_PATH=/the_extracted_cusparselt_path`: 222 223 ```bash 224 wget https://developer.download.nvidia.com/compute/libcusparse-lt/0.1.0/local_installers/libcusparse_lt-linux-x86_64-0.1.0.2.tar.gz 225 tar -xzvf libcusparse_lt-linux-x86_64-0.1.0.2.tar.gz 226 cmake -DSM=xx -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release -DBUILD_PYT=ON -DSPARSITY_SUPPORT=ON -DCUSPARSELT_PATH=/the_extracted_cusparselt_path .. 227 make -j12 228 ``` 229 230 ## How to use 231 232 ### Run FasterTransformer BERT on C++ 233 234 1. Generate the `gemm_config.in` file for FP32/FP16/BF16 and the `igemm_config.in` file for INT8 235 236 The generation of best GEMM configuration is recommended no matter what platform we use when we use FasterTransformer. If we do not generate the configure file, the FasterTransformer will use the default configuration and the inference speed may be slower. 237 238 Assume the settings of the BERT are as follows: 239 240 - `batch_size`=32 241 - `sequence_length`=32 242 - `head_number`=12 243 - `size_per_head`=64 244 - `data_type`=0 (FP32) or 1 (FP16) or 2 (BF16) 245 - `int8_mode`=0 246 247 Then the following scripts can generate the best GEMM configuration under such settings and record the configuration into the `gemm_config.in` file. 248 249 ```bash 250 ./bin/bert_gemm 32 32 12 64 0 0 1 0 251 ``` 252 253 If the application may have multiple different shapes (like different batch size), users can run multiple time and set `is_append` to be true. For example 254 255 ```bash 256 ./bin/bert_gemm 32 32 12 64 0 0 1 0 # bs 32, not append, will create a new gemm_config.ini 257 ./bin/bert_gemm 64 32 12 64 0 0 1 1 # bs 64, append results in existed gemm_config.ini 258 ``` 259 260 In the following subsection, we use the same settings and 12 transformer layers unless specified. 261 262 2. Run FasterTransformer BERT under FP32 on C++ 263 264 `./bin/bert_example` runs the BERT in the `C++`. The arguments of `bert_example` is: 265 266 ```bash 267 ./bin/bert_example 268 ``` 269 Data Type = 0 (FP32) or 1 (FP16) or 2 (BF16) 270 271 Then the following scripts can run the BERT under the above settings. 272 273 ```bash 274 ./bin/bert_example 32 12 32 12 64 0 0 275 ``` 276 277 The outputs should be like to the following: 278 279 ```bash 280 Device Tesla V100-PCIE-32GB 281 before allocate free 29.46 GB total 31.75 GB 282 After allocate free 29.41 GB used 2.34 GB total 31.75 GB 283 [INFO] batch_size 32 seq_len 32 layer 12 FT-CPP-time 16.51 ms (100 iterations) 284 ``` 285 286 3. Run FasterTransformer BERT under FP16/BF16 on C++ 287 288 So far, we use the FP32 to run the FasterTransformer. If we use the volta or newer NVIDIA GPU, we can use tensor core when we use the FP16. BF16 is only supported after Ampere NVIDIA GPU (SM 80). 289 290 To use the FP16, we only need to set the `` flag to 1 like following: 291 292 ```bash 293 ./bin/bert_gemm 32 32 12 64 1 0 294 ./bin/bert_example 32 12 32 12 64 1 0 0 295 ``` 296 297 To use the BF16, we only need to set the `` flag to 2. 298 299 Note that the configuration of FP32 and FP16/BF16 are different, so we need to generate the configuration again. 300 301 The outputs should be like to the following: 302 303 ```bash 304 Device Tesla V100-PCIE-32GB 305 before allocate free 29.46 GB total 31.75 GB 306 After allocate free 29.43 GB used 2.32 GB total 31.75 GB 307 [INFO] batch_size 32 seq_len 32 layer 12 FT-CPP-time 4.00 ms 308 ``` 309 310 4. Run FasterTransformer BERT under INT8 on C++ 311 312 If we use the Turing or newer NVIDIA GPUs, we can use tensor core when we use the INT8. 313 314 To use the INT8, we only need to set the `` flag to 1 or 2 like following: 315 316 ```bash 317 #For int8_mode == 1 318 ./bin/bert_gemm 32 32 12 64 1 1 319 ./bin/bert_int8_example 32 12 32 12 64 1 0 1 320 321 #For int8_mode == 2 322 ./bin/bert_gemm 32 32 12 64 1 2 323 ./bin/bert_int8_example 32 12 32 12 64 1 0 2 324 ``` 325 326 Note that the configuration file of FP32/FP16 and INT8 are different, so we need to generate the configuration again. And the is not necessarily 1, depending on what data type bias is. 327 328 The outputs should be like to the following: 329 330 ```bash 331 #For int8_mode == 1 332 Device Tesla T4 333 Device Tesla T4 334 before allocate free 14.64 GB total 14.76 GB 335 After allocate free 14.62 GB used 0.13 GB total 14.76 GB 336 [INFO] batch_size 32 seq_len 32 layer 12 FT-CPP-time 7.49 ms ( 50 iterations) 337 338 #For int8_mode == 2 339 Device Tesla T4 340 Device Tesla T4 341 before allocate free 14.64 GB total 14.76 GB 342 After allocate free 14.62 GB used 0.13 GB total 14.76 GB 343 [INFO] batch_size 32 seq_len 32 layer 12 FT-CPP-time 4.79 ms ( 50 iterations) 344 ``` 345 346 5. Run Effective FasterTransformer under FP32 on C++ 347 348 To use the Effective FasterTransformer, we only need to set the `` flag to 1 like following: 349 350 ```bash 351 ./bin/bert_gemm 32 32 12 64 0 0 352 ./bin/bert_example 32 12 32 12 64 0 1 0 353 ``` 354 355 The outputs should be like to the following: 356 357 ```bash 358 Device Tesla V100-PCIE-32GB 359 before allocate free 29.46 GB total 31.75 GB 360 After allocate free 29.40 GB used 2.35 GB total 31.75 GB 361 [INFO] batch_size 32 seq_len 32 layer 12 FT-CPP-time 9.77 ms 362 ``` 363 364 6. Run Effective FasterTransformer under INT8 on C++ 365 366 To use the Effective FasterTransformer under INT8, we need to set the `` flag to 1 and `` flag to 1 or 2 like following. Since the sequence length in INT8 should be a multiple of 32, Effective FasterTransformer could be a good choice for INT8. 367 368 ```bash 369 #For int8_mode == 1 370 ./bin/bert_gemm 32 32 12 64 1 1 371 ./bin/bert_int8_example 32 12 32 12 64 1 1 1 372 373 #For int8_mode == 2 374 ./bin/bert_gemm 32 32 12 64 1 2 375 ./bin/bert_int8_example 32 12 32 12 64 1 1 2 376 ``` 377 378 The outputs should be like to the following: 379 380 ```bash 381 #For int8_mode == 1 382 Device Tesla T4 383 Device Tesla T4 384 before allocate free 14.64 GB total 14.76 GB 385 After allocate free 14.62 GB used 0.14 GB total 14.76 GB 386 [INFO] batch_size 32 seq_len 32 layer 12 FT-CPP-time 4.06 ms ( 50 iterations) 387 388 389 #For int8_mode == 2 390 Device Tesla T4 391 Device Tesla T4 392 before allocate free 14.64 GB total 14.76 GB 393 After allocate free 14.62 GB used 0.14 GB total 14.76 GB 394 [INFO] batch_size 32 seq_len 32 layer 12 FT-CPP-time 2.69 ms ( 50 iterations) 395 ``` 396 397 7. Run FasterTransformer under FP8 on C++ (Experimental) 398 399 Note that FP8 is supported since Hopper and CUDA 11.8. Here, we use docker image `nvcr.io/nvidia/pytorch:22.10-py3` to demonstrate 400 401 ```bash 402 nvidia-docker run -ti --shm-size 5g --rm nvcr.io/nvidia/pytorch:22.10-py3 bash 403 git clone https://github.com/NVIDIA/FasterTransformer.git 404 mkdir -p FasterTransformer/build 405 cd FasterTransformer/build 406 git submodule init && git submodule update 407 cmake -DSM=90 -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release -DENABLE_FP8=ON .. 408 make -j12 409 git lfs clone https://huggingface.co/bert-base-uncased 410 python3 ../examples/pytorch/bert/utils/huggingface_bert_convert.py -i bert-base-uncased/ -o bert-base-uncased/ft_fp32/ -i_g 1 411 python3 ../examples/pytorch/bert/utils/huggingface_bert_fp8_convert.py -i bert-base-uncased/ -o bert-base-uncased/ft_fp8/ 412 ./bin/bert_fp8_example 4 12 384 12 64 1 1 ./bert-base-uncased/ 413 ``` 414 415 ### Run FasterTransformer BERT on TensorFlow 416 417 1. Run FasterTransformer encoder under FP32 on TensorFlow 418 419 ```bash 420 ./bin/bert_gemm 32 32 12 64 0 0 421 python ../examples/tensorflow/bert/bert_example.py \ 422 --batch_size 32 \ 423 --max_seq_len 32 \ 424 --head_number 12 \ 425 --size_per_head 64 \ 426 --num_layer 12 \ 427 --data_type fp32 \ 428 --test_time 1 429 ``` 430 431 The outputs should be like to the following: 432 433 ```bash 434 [INFO] Encoder TF v.s. FT with tensor input Cross check True 435 [INFO] Max diff 5.4836273193359375e-06 436 [INFO] min diff 0.0 437 [INFO] Encoder TF v.s. EFF-FT with tensor input Cross check True 438 [INFO] Max diff 4.76837158203125e-06 439 [INFO] min diff 0.0 440 [INFO] batch_size 32 max_seq_len 32 precision FP32 12 layer TF-while-time 44.49 ms ( 50 iterations) 441 [INFO] batch_size 32 max_seq_len 32 precision FP32 12 layer FT-OP-while-time 44.30 ms ( 50 iterations) 442 [INFO] batch_size 32 max_seq_len 32 precision FP32 12 layer EFF-OP-while-time 22.83 ms ( 50 iterations) 443 ``` 444 445 Note: We can also generate the best GEMM configuration when running encoder by setting `--allow_gemm_test True`. 446 Note: If users use Ampere GPUs, then TensorFlow will uses TF32 by default, and hence TensorFlow will be faster than FasterTransformer, and have large value differences. 447 448 2. Run FasterTransformer for GLUE dataset 537 538 This subsection demonstrates how to integrate the FasterTransformer in TensorFlow and evaluate the accuracy of FasterTransformer on GLUE dataset. To evaluate on GLUE dataset, it requires the repo of [BERT](https://github.com/google-research/bert). 539 540 1. Prepare the BERT codes, Download the BERT pretrained model. 541 542 ```bash 543 git clone https://github.com/google-research/bert.git tensorflow/tensorflow_bert/bert 544 wget https://storage.googleapis.com/bert_models/2018_10_18/uncased_L-12_H-768_A-12.zip 545 unzip uncased_L-12_H-768_A-12.zip 546 ``` 547 548 2. Download the GLUE MRPC dataset. Note that the file `download_glue_data.py` can only executed under python3. 549 550 ```bash 551 wget https://gist.githubusercontent.com/W4ngatang/60c2bdb54d156a41194446737ce03e2e/raw/1502038877f6a88c225a34450793fbc3ea87eaba/download_glue_data.py 552 python download_glue_data.py --tasks MRPC 553 ``` 554 555 Note: If the `download_glue_data.py` has some issues, try to use [this](https://gist.github.com/vlasenkoalexey/fef1601580f269eca73bf26a198595f3). 556 557 3. Finetune the pretrained model on MRPC datasets. This takes some minutes. 558 559 ```bash 560 export BERT_BASE_DIR=${PWD}/uncased_L-12_H-768_A-12 561 export GLUE_DIR=${PWD}/glue_data/ 562 563 python ../examples/tensorflow/bert/tensorflow_bert/bert/run_classifier.py \ 564 --task_name=MRPC \ 565 --do_train=true \ 566 --do_eval=true \ 567 --data_dir=$GLUE_DIR/MRPC \ 568 --vocab_file=$BERT_BASE_DIR/vocab.txt \ 569 --bert_config_file=$BERT_BASE_DIR/bert_config.json \ 570 --init_checkpoint=$BERT_BASE_DIR/bert_model.ckpt \ 571 --max_seq_length=128 \ 572 --train_batch_size=32 \ 573 --learning_rate=2e-5 \ 574 --num_train_epochs=3.0 \ 575 --output_dir=mrpc_output/ 576 ``` 577 578 The results would be like: 579 580 ```bash 581 INFO:tensorflow:***** Eval results ***** 582 I1204 01:22:58.318809 139767509075776 run_classifier.py:923] ***** Eval results ***** 583 INFO:tensorflow: eval_accuracy = 0.84068626 584 I1204 01:22:58.318926 139767509075776 run_classifier.py:925] eval_accuracy = 0.84068626 585 INFO:tensorflow: eval_loss = 0.5026195 586 I1204 01:22:58.319309 139767509075776 run_classifier.py:925] eval_loss = 0.5026195 587 INFO:tensorflow: global_step = 343 588 I1204 01:22:58.319446 139767509075776 run_classifier.py:925] global_step = 343 589 INFO:tensorflow: loss = 0.5026195 590 I1204 01:22:58.319561 139767509075776 run_classifier.py:925] loss = 0.5026195 591 592 ``` 593 594 4. Evaluate the accuracy of FasterTransformer under FP32 595 596 To evaluate the accuracy of FasterTransformer, we can use `tensorflow/tensorflow_bert/run_classifier_wrap.py`. This file uses `run_classifier.py` of BERT repo, replacing the transformer model by FasterTransformer and add some additional arguments like `--floatx`. 597 598 ```bash 599 ./bin/bert_gemm 8 128 12 64 0 0 600 python ../examples/tensorflow/bert/tensorflow_bert/run_classifier_wrap.py \ 601 --floatx=float32 \ 602 --task_name=MRPC \ 603 --do_eval=true \ 604 --data_dir=$GLUE_DIR/MRPC \ 605 --vocab_file=$BERT_BASE_DIR/vocab.txt \ 606 --bert_config_file=$BERT_BASE_DIR/bert_config.json \ 607 --init_checkpoint=mrpc_output/model.ckpt-343 \ 608 --max_seq_length=128 \ 609 --eval_batch_size=8 \ 610 --output_dir=mrpc_output 611 ``` 612 613 The results would be like: 614 615 ```bash 616 INFO:tensorflow:***** Eval results ***** 617 I1204 01:25:59.202495 140314814412608 run_classifier.py:923] ***** Eval results ***** 618 INFO:tensorflow: eval_accuracy = 0.84068626 619 I1204 01:25:59.202617 140314814412608 run_classifier.py:925] eval_accuracy = 0.84068626 620 INFO:tensorflow: eval_loss = 0.50261945 621 I1204 01:25:59.202857 140314814412608 run_classifier.py:925] eval_loss = 0.50261945 622 INFO:tensorflow: global_step = 343 623 I1204 01:25:59.202969 140314814412608 run_classifier.py:925] global_step = 343 624 INFO:tensorflow: loss = 0.50261945 625 I1204 01:25:59.203072 140314814412608 run_classifier.py:925] loss = 0.50261945 626 ``` 627 628 5. Convert the finetuned checkpoint to FP16 and evaluate the accuracy of FasterTransformer under FP16. 629 630 To convert the checkpoint from FP32 to FP16, we can use `../examples/tensorflow/bert/tensorflow_bert/ckpt_type_convert.py` to convert the checkpoint. This file requires two arguments, the location of FP32 checkpoint, and the location putting the FP16 checkpoint. 631 632 ```bash 633 python ../examples/tensorflow/bert/tensorflow_bert/ckpt_type_convert.py \ 634 --init_checkpoint=mrpc_output/model.ckpt-343 \ 635 --fp16_checkpoint=mrpc_output_fp16/fp16_model.ckpt 636 ./bin/bert_gemm 8 128 12 64 1 0 637 python ../examples/tensorflow/bert/tensorflow_bert/run_classifier_wrap.py \ 638 --floatx=float16 \ 639 --task_name=MRPC \ 640 --do_eval=true \ 641 --data_dir=$GLUE_DIR/MRPC \ 642 --vocab_file=$BERT_BASE_DIR/vocab.txt \ 643 --bert_config_file=$BERT_BASE_DIR/bert_config.json \ 644 --init_checkpoint=mrpc_output_fp16/fp16_model.ckpt \ 645 --max_seq_length=128 \ 646 --eval_batch_size=8 \ 647 --output_dir=mrpc_output_fp16 648 ``` 649 650 The results would be like: 651 652 ```bash 653 INFO:tensorflow:***** Eval results ***** 654 I1204 01:27:49.961906 139736813242176 run_classifier.py:923] ***** Eval results ***** 655 INFO:tensorflow: eval_accuracy = 0.8504902 656 I1204 01:27:49.962020 139736813242176 run_classifier.py:925] eval_accuracy = 0.8504902 657 INFO:tensorflow: eval_loss = 0.5103425 658 I1204 01:27:49.962350 139736813242176 run_classifier.py:925] eval_loss = 0.5103425 659 INFO:tensorflow: global_step = 343 660 I1204 01:27:49.962482 139736813242176 run_classifier.py:925] global_step = 343 661 INFO:tensorflow: loss = 0.5103358 662 I1204 01:27:49.962604 139736813242176 run_classifier.py:925] loss = 0.5103358 663 ``` 664 665 6. Compare the speed of BERT of TensorFlow and FasterTransformer under both FP32 and FP16. 666 667 To compare the speed of TensorFlow and FasterTransformer on BERT model directly, we can use `../examples/tensorflow/bert/tensorflow_bert/profile_transformer_inferece.py`. 668 669 ```bash 670 ./bin/bert_gemm 8 128 12 64 0 0 671 python ../examples/tensorflow/bert/tensorflow_bert/profile_transformer_inference.py \ 672 --init_checkpoint=mrpc_output/model.ckpt-343 \ 673 --tf_profile=false \ 674 --output_dir=mrpc_output \ 675 --profiling_output_file=time_elapsed \ 676 --xla=true \ 677 --floatx=float32 678 ./bin/bert_gemm 8 128 12 64 1 0 679 python ../examples/tensorflow/bert/tensorflow_bert/profile_transformer_inference.py \ 680 --init_checkpoint=mrpc_output_fp16/fp16_model.ckpt \ 681 --tf_profile=false \ 682 --output_dir=mrpc_output_fp16 \ 683 --profiling_output_file=time_elapsed \ 684 --xla=true \ 685 --floatx=float16 686 ``` 687 688 The results of FP32 would be like: 689 690 ```bash 691 average time (seconds) elapsed original tensorflow: 0.049444806575775144 sec 692 average time (seconds) elapsed fast transformer: 0.046385574340820315 sec 693 ``` 694 695 The results of FP16 would be like: 696 697 ```bash 698 average time (seconds) elapsed original tensorflow: 0.015163004398345947 sec 699 average time (seconds) elapsed fast transformer: 0.009342837333679199 sec 700 ``` 701 702 5. Run FasterTransformer for SQuAD 1.1 dataset 703 704 This subsection demonstrates how to integrate the FasterTransformer in TensorFlow and evaluates the accuracy of FasterTransformer on SQuAD 1.1 dataset. To evaluate on SQuAD 1.1 dataset, it requires the repo of [BERT](https://github.com/google-research/bert). 705 706 1. Prepare the BERT codes and download the fine-tuned model of SQuAD 1.1 from NGC 707 708 Because the training time of SQuAD is longer, and the NVIDIA NGC has provided the fine-tuned BERT model, we download the fine-tuned model directly. 709 710 ```bash 711 git clone https://github.com/google-research/bert.git tensorflow/tensorflow_bert/bert 712 wget --content-disposition https://api.ngc.nvidia.com/v2/models/nvidia/bert_tf_ckpt_base_qa_squad11_amp_128/versions/19.03.1/zip -O bert_tf_ckpt_base_qa_squad11_amp_128_19.03.1.zip 713 unzip bert_tf_ckpt_base_qa_squad11_amp_128_19.03.1.zip -d squad_model 714 ``` 715 716 2. Download the SQuAD dataset. 717 718 ```bash 719 mkdir squad_data 720 wget -P squad_data https://rajpurkar.github.io/SQuAD-explorer/dataset/train-v1.1.json 721 wget -P squad_data https://rajpurkar.github.io/SQuAD-explorer/dataset/dev-v1.1.json 722 ``` 723 724 3. Evaluate the accuracy of TensorFlow under FP32 725 726 ```bash 727 python ../examples/tensorflow/bert/tensorflow_bert/bert/run_squad.py \ 728 --predict_batch_size=8 \ 729 --vocab_file=squad_model/vocab.txt \ 730 --bert_config_file=squad_model/bert_config.json \ 731 --init_checkpoint=squad_model/model.ckpt \ 732 --train_file=squad_data/train-v1.1.json \ 733 --do_predict=True \ 734 --predict_file=squad_data/dev-v1.1.json \ 735 --max_seq_length=384 \ 736 --output_dir=./squad_tf_output/fp_32/ 737 738 python ../examples/tensorflow/bert/tensorflow_bert/squad_evaluate_v1_1.py squad_data/dev-v1.1.json squad_tf_output/fp_32/predictions.json 739 ``` 740 741 The results of TensorFlow would be like: 742 743 ```bash 744 {"exact_match": 78.9120151371807, "f1": 86.22012390507868} 745 ``` 746 747 4. Evaluate the accuracy of FasterTransformer under FP32 748 749 To evaluate the accuracy of FasterTransformer, we can use `../examples/tensorflow/bert/tensorflow_bert/run_squad_wrap.py`. This file uses `run_squad.py` of BERT repo, replacing the transformer model by FasterTransformer, and add some additional arguments like `--floatx`. 750 751 ```bash 752 ./bin/bert_gemm 8 384 12 64 0 0 753 python ../examples/tensorflow/bert/tensorflow_bert/run_squad_wrap.py \ 754 --floatx=float32 \ 755 --predict_batch_size=8 \ 756 --vocab_file=squad_model/vocab.txt \ 757 --bert_config_file=squad_model/bert_config.json \ 758 --init_checkpoint=squad_model/model.ckpt \ 759 --train_file=squad_data/train-v1.1.json \ 760 --do_predict=True \ 761 --predict_file=squad_data/dev-v1.1.json \ 762 --max_seq_length=384 \ 763 --output_dir=./squad_ft_output/fp_32/ \ 764 --allow_gemm_test=False 765 766 python ../examples/tensorflow/bert/tensorflow_bert/squad_evaluate_v1_1.py squad_data/dev-v1.1.json squad_ft_output/fp_32/predictions.json 767 ``` 768 769 The results of TensorFlow would be like: 770 771 ```bash 772 {"exact_match": 78.9120151371807, "f1": 86.22012390507868} 773 ``` 774 775 5. Convert the checkpoint to FP16 and evaluate the accuracy of TensorFlow and FasterTransformer under FP16 776 777 To convert the checkpoint from FP32 to FP16, we can use `tensorflow/tensorflow_bert/ckpt_type_convert.py` to convert the checkpoint. This file requires two arguments, the location of FP32 checkpoint, and the location putting the FP16 checkpoint. 778 779 ```bash 780 python ../examples/tensorflow/ckpt_type_convert.py --init_checkpoint=squad_model/model.ckpt --fp16_checkpoint=squad_fp16_model/model.ckpt 781 782 ./bin/bert_gemm 8 384 12 64 1 0 783 python ../examples/tensorflow/bert/tensorflow_bert/run_squad_wrap.py \ 784 --floatx=float16 \ 785 --predict_batch_size=8 \ 786 --vocab_file=squad_model/vocab.txt \ 787 --bert_config_file=squad_model/bert_config.json \ 788 --init_checkpoint=squad_fp16_model/model.ckpt \ 789 --train_file=squad_data/train-v1.1.json \ 790 --do_predict=True \ 791 --predict_file=squad_data/dev-v1.1.json \ 792 --max_seq_length=384 \ 793 --output_dir=./squad_ft_output/fp_16/ \ 794 --allow_gemm_test=False 795 796 python ../examples/tensorflow/bert/tensorflow_bert/squad_evaluate_v1_1.py squad_data/dev-v1.1.json squad_ft_output/fp_16/predictions.json 797 ``` 798 799 The results of TensorFlow would be like: 800 801 ```bash 802 {"exact_match": 79.03500473036897, "f1": 86.23027825772257} 803 ``` 804 805 6. Evaluate the accuracy of Effective FasterTransformer under FP16 806 807 Since the total sequence length is not fixed, we recommend using the default gemm configuration directly for Effective FasterTransformer. 808 809 ```bash 810 python ../examples/tensorflow/bert/tensorflow_bert/run_squad_wrap.py \ 811 --floatx=float16 \ 812 --predict_batch_size=8 \ 813 --vocab_file=squad_model/vocab.txt \ 814 --bert_config_file=squad_model/bert_config.json \ 815 --init_checkpoint=squad_fp16_model/model.ckpt \ 816 --train_file=squad_data/train-v1.1.json \ 817 --do_predict=True \ 818 --predict_file=squad_data/dev-v1.1.json \ 819 --max_seq_length=384 \ 820 --output_dir=./squad_ft_output/fp_16/ \ 821 --allow_gemm_test=False \ 822 --remove_padding=True 823 824 python ../examples/tensorflow/bert/tensorflow_bert/squad_evaluate_v1_1.py squad_data/dev-v1.1.json squad_ft_output/fp_16/predictions.json 825 ``` 826 827 The results of TensorFlow would be like: 828 829 ```bash 830 {"exact_match": 79.04446546830653, "f1": 86.23343183703513} 831 ``` 832 833 7. Evaluate the accuracy of FasterTransformer under INT8 834 835 Please refer to the directory `examples/tensorflow/bert/bert-quantization` first for how to get a quantized model. In `section 5.7` and `section 5.8`, to keep consistent with the procedures described in `examples/tensorflow/bert/bert-quantization`, we use `https://storage.googleapis.com/bert_models/2020_02_20/uncased_L-12_H-768_A-12.zip` as initial checkpoint with finetuned accuracy of == <89.57, 82.44>. 836 837 In `bert-tf-quantization`, we give detailed procedure of Post Training Quantization (PTQ), Quantization Aware Training (QAT) and QAT with Knowledge-distillation. Since they have the same inference procedure, we use QAT-KD checkpoint to show how to evaluate the accuracy of FasterTransformer under INT8. 838 839 Suppose we already fine-tuned a FP32 checkpoint using QAT-KD with int8_mode == 2 as described in `bert-quantization`. The path to checkpoint is `squad_model/QAT_KD_mode_2/`. 840 841 We first convert the checkpoint from FP32 to FP16 (this step is not necessary, but it will give us a better performance) and then quantize the FP16 checkpoint using `../examples/tensorflow/bert/tensorflow_bert/ckpt_quantization.py`. This file requires three arguments, the location of initial checkpoint, the location putting the quantized checkpoint and the int8_mode. 842 843 ```bash 844 python ../examples/tensorflow/bert/tensorflow_bert/ckpt_type_convert.py --init_checkpoint=squad_model/QAT_KD_mode_2/model.ckpt-27374 --fp16_checkpoint=squad_model/QAT_KD_mode_2_fp16/model.ckpt 845 846 python ../examples/tensorflow/bert/tensorflow_bert/ckpt_quantization.py --init_checkpoint=squad_model/QAT_KD_mode_2_fp16/model.ckpt --quantized_checkpoint=squad_model/QAT_KD_mode_2_fp16_quantized/model.ckpt --int8_mode=2 847 848 ./bin/bert_gemm 8 384 12 64 1 1 849 python ../examples/tensorflow/bert/tensorflow_bert/run_squad_wrap.py \ 850 --floatx=float16 \ 851 --predict_batch_size=8 \ 852 --vocab_file=squad_model/vocab.txt \ 853 --bert_config_file=squad_model/bert_config.json \ 854 --init_checkpoint=squad_model/QAT_KD_mode_2_fp16_quantized/model.ckpt \ 855 --train_file=squad_data/train-v1.1.json \ 856 --do_predict=True \ 857 --predict_file=squad_data/dev-v1.1.json \ 858 --max_seq_length=384 \ 859 --output_dir=./squad_ft_output/int8_mode_2/ \ 860 --int8_mode=2 \ 861 --allow_gemm_test=False 862 863 python ../examples/tensorflow/bert/tensorflow_bert/squad_evaluate_v1_1.py squad_data/dev-v1.1.json squad_ft_output/int8_mode_2/predictions.json 864 ``` 865 866 The results of TensorFlow would be like: 867 868 ```bash 869 {"exact_match": 83.85052034058657, "f1": 90.46351799300075} 870 ``` 871 872 8. Evaluate the accuracy of Effective FasterTransformer under INT8 873 874 To evaluate the accuracy of Effective FasterTransformer under INT8, we follow the steps described in above section to get the correct checkpoint, and then run `../examples/tensorflow/bert/tensorflow_bert/run_squad_wrap.py` with `--remove_padding True` flag. 875 876 ```bash 877 ./bin/bert_gemm 8 384 12 64 1 1 878 python ../examples/tensorflow/bert/tensorflow_bert/run_squad_wrap.py \ 879 --floatx=float16 \ 880 --predict_batch_size=8 \ 881 --vocab_file=squad_model/vocab.txt \ 882 --bert_config_file=squad_model/bert_config.json \ 883 --init_checkpoint=squad_model/QAT_KD_mode_2_fp16_quantized/model.ckpt \ 884 --train_file=squad_data/train-v1.1.json \ 885 --do_predict=True \ 886 --predict_file=squad_data/dev-v1.1.json \ 887 --max_seq_length=384 \ 888 --output_dir=./squad_ft_output/int8_mode_2_effectiveTransformer/ \ 889 --remove_padding=True \ 890 --int8_mode=2 \ 891 --allow_gemm_test=False 892 893 python ../examples/tensorflow/bert/tensorflow_bert/squad_evaluate_v1_1.py squad_data/dev-v1.1.json squad_ft_output/int8_mode_2_effectiveTransformer/predictions.json 894 ``` 895 896 The results of TensorFlow would be like: 897 898 ```bash 899 {"exact_match": 83.85052034058657, "f1": 90.46351799300075} 900 ``` 901 902 ### Run FasterTransformer BERT on PyTorch 903 904 Please install HuggingFace's `transformers` first before running the demos by 905 906 ```bash 907 pip install transformers==2.5.1 908 ``` 909 910 1. Generate the `gemm_config.in` file: 911 912 ```bash 913 ./bin/bert_gemm 914 ./bin/bert_gemm 1 32 12 64 1 0 915 ``` 916 Data Type = 0 (FP32) or 1 (FP16) or 2 (BF16) 917 918 If you want to use the library in other directory, please generate this file according to your setting and copy it to your working directory. 919 920 2. Run the PyTorch BERT sample: 921 922 ```bash 923 python ../examples/pytorch/bert/bert_example.py <--data_type fp32/fp16/bf16> <--int8_mode 0/1/2/3> <--sparse> <--time> 924 python ../examples/pytorch/bert/bert_example.py 1 12 32 12 64 --data_type fp16 --time 925 ``` 926 927 Set `--data_type fp32` for fp32 mode, and set `--data_type bf16` for bf16 mode. `--int8_mode 1` or `--int8_mode 2` or `--int8_mode 3` will use int8_mode 1 or 2 or 3 in FasterTransformer. `--sparse` will use Ampere sparsity feature if FasterTransformer is built with sparsity support. 928 929 The outputs should be like to the following: 930 931 ```bash 932 FT Mean diff: 0.0004119873046875 933 FT Max diff: 0.00830078125 934 FT Min diff: 0.0 935 EFF-FT Mean diff: 0.0004119873046875 936 EFF-FT Max diff: 0.00830078125 937 EFF-FT Min diff: 0.0 938 [INFO] HuggingFaceEnocder time costs: 5.77 ms 939 [INFO] FasterTransformer time costs: 1.12 ms 940 [INFO] EFF-FasterTransformer time costs: 1.33 ms 941 ``` 942 943 3. Run the BERT MRPC sample code: 944
